message.content: Adversaries may install SSL/TLS digital certificates to facilitate secure communications during their operations. These certificates, which authenticate the identity of the certificate holder and enable encrypted communication, can be used to encrypt command and control (C2) traffic or lend credibility to malicious sites, such as those used for credential harvesting. Common exploitation methods include obtaining legitimate certificates or creating self-signed ones, and installing them on compromised or adversary-controlled infrastructure, such as web or email servers. The potential impact includes enhanced stealth and credibility for malicious activities, making detection and interception more challenging. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring for unauthorized certificate installations, employing strict certificate management policies, and using network traffic analysis to detect anomalies indicative of malicious SSL/TLS usage.
